attributes.stem:
A 52-year-old man is admitted to the hospital ward following a motorcycle accident two days ago. At the time of the trauma he was reviewed in the Emergency Department and found to have multiple minor soft tissue injuries (including small abrasions and bruising on left arm and both legs) and a small pneumothorax which has been conservatively managed in hospital. The duty intern is called to review him due to increasing right leg pain. He has a medical history of type 2 diabetes. His only regular medication is metformin, and he has been taking paracetamol and ibuprofen as needed since the accident.

On examination, he appears unwell with a pulse rate of 110/min, blood pressure 95/60 mmHg, respiratory rate 24/min, SpO2 94% on room air, and temperature 38.5°C. His right leg is swollen and tender, with areas of dark discolouration. A frothy exudate can be seen exuding from the wound site. The patient reports increasing pain and weakness in the affected limb.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
attributes.explanation:
This scenario presents a patient with signs and symptoms highly suggestive of a severe anaerobic infection, likely clostridial myonecrosis (gas gangrene), which is a result of Clostridium perfringens infection. The patient's presentation with fever, tachycardia, hypotension, and tachypnoea indicates systemic involvement. The physical examination findings of dark discolouration and frothy exudate are classic signs of anaerobic infection, particularly clostridial myonecrosis. These infections can progress rapidly, leading to severe systemic toxicity and potentially fatal outcomes if not treated promptly.

Urgent surgical referral is the most appropriate next step in management. Gas gangrene is a surgical emergency requiring immediate consultation with a surgeon for urgent debridement. This should be accompanied by immediate commencement of intravenous antibiotics (benzylpenicillin plus clindamycin or lincomycin). The surgical team will perform aggressive removal of all necrotic and infected tissue, which eliminates the source of toxin production and bacterial proliferation. Delay in surgical intervention significantly increases mortality.

Fasciotomy is performed to relieve compartment syndrome, which can occur with severe soft tissue infections. While it may be necessary in some cases, it addresses only the increased compartment pressure and not the underlying infection. Fasciotomy alone would not remove the necrotic tissue that is the source of ongoing infection and toxin production. This would be a surgical decision made after assessment.

Intravenous metronidazole and lincomycin is an alternative antibiotic regimen that may be used in patients with penicillin hypersensitivity, where metronidazole replaces benzylpenicillin.

Hyperbaric oxygen therapy has been used as an adjunctive treatment for gas gangrene, as increased tissue oxygenation can inhibit anaerobic bacterial growth. However, it is not a substitute for surgical debridement and should never delay definitive surgical management. Its efficacy remains controversial, and it is not universally available.

Intravenous benzylpenicillin and metronidazole represents an incorrect antibiotic combination for gas gangrene. While benzylpenicillin is appropriate, it should be combined with clindamycin or lincomycin rather than metronidazole.
learningPoints: Gas gangrene requires both urgent surgical debridement and appropriate intravenous antibiotic therapy.
Intravenous benzylpenicillin plus clindamycin (or lincomycin) is the antibiotic regimen of choice for gas gangrene, with metronidazole as an alternative for penicillin-allergic patients.
references: Bryant AE, Stevens DL. Clostridial Myonecrosis: New Insights in Pathogenesis and Management. Current Infectious Disease Reports. 2010;12(5):383-91. Available at: https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11908-010-0127-y

Yang Z, Hu J, Qu Y, Sun F, Leng X, Li H, Zhan S. Interventions for treating gas gangrene. Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews. 2015;12. Available at:https://www.cochranelibrary.com/cdsr/doi/10.1002/14651858.CD010577.pub2/full

Devaney B, Frawley G, Frawley L, Pilcher D. Necrotising soft tissue infections: the effect of hyperbaric oxygen on mortality. Anaesth Intensive Care. 2015;43(6):685-692. Available at: https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/pdf/10.1177/0310057X1504300604

Raju A, Swamy S, Ghosh P. ROole of hyerpbaric oxygen therapy in treating a case of clostridial myonecrosis (gas gangrene). Indian Journal of Aerospace Medicine. 2017;61(2):20-23. Available at: https://indjaerospacemed.com/role-of-hyperbaric-oxygen-therapy-in-treating-a-case-of-clostridial-myonecrosis-gas-gangrene/

Willems A, Shayan R. Necrotising soft tissue infection: A diagnosis not to be missed. Aust J Gen Pract. 2021 Aug;50(8):567-569. Available at: https://www1.racgp.org.au/ajgp/2021/august/necrotising-soft-tissue-infection
Therapeutic Guidelines Ltd. Necrotising skin and soft tissue infections. In: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic, March 2025 update [Internet]. Melbourne: Therapeutic Guidelines Ltd; 2025.
